text: Total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis is one treatment option for familial adenomatous polyposis requiring surgery. We have developed a method of performing this procedure using completely intracorporeal laparoscopic techniques. Important features of this technique include proximal intracorporeal ligation of named mesenteric vessels, use of a novel retraction device for mobilization of the left colon and splenic flexure, removal of the specimen in preparation of the terminal ileum for anastomosis through an enlarged suprapubic cannula incision, and intracorporeal iliorectal anastomosis utilizing a standard double staple technique. The patient is placed supine in the modified lithotomy position using specially designed stirrups. The laparoscope is placed through an umbilical cannula and five additional cannula are placed as seen here.

During phase one of the colectomy, the assistant retracts the mesosigmoid anteriorly while the surgeon incises the peritoneum to the right of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The IMA is swept anteriorly away from the preaortic hypogastric neural plexus using blunt dissection. The dissection continues medially beneath the IMA and superior rectal artery, identifying and sweeping the left ureter and gonadal vessels posteriorly. A peritoneal window is created lateral to the IMA and vein, and the vascular pedicle is draped over the grasper. The inferior mesenteric artery and vein are ligated below the left colic artery using an endoscopic vascular stapler, ensuring the left ureter is safely retracted.

The surgeon continues dissection in the retroperitoneum, elevating the mesosigmoid over to the lateral abdominal wall. The sigmoid colon is mobilized past the sacral promontory, and the thin lateral peritoneal attachments are taken down. The sigmoid colon is now fully mobilized. A team member identifies the distal resection line on the rectum using a rigid proctoscope. Dissection of the mesorectum begins on the right, with larger vessels managed using clips or an endoscopic linear stapler. The rectum is divided with two applications of a three-centimeter endoscopic stapler.

The assistant passes an endoscopic snare through the right upper quadrant cannula, pulling the cut end of the colon through the loop with a grasper. The colon distal to the loop is flipped over the snare retractor shaft to expose posterior mesenteric attachments. The surgeon dissects cephalad, dividing lateral peritoneal attachments in the left colonic mesentery sharply. Girota's fascia is swept from the posterior surface of the colonic mesentery, and the base of the left mesocolon is divided. The snare retractor is moved up the colon for traction, and the splenic flexure is taken down by dividing peritoneal attachments close to the bowel. The bowel is retracted to the right, exposing the proper plane, and the greater omentum is separated from the colon using sharp dissection in the avascular plane close to the bowel wall, moving from left to right.

The surgeon removes the colon from the snare retractor and places it into the left abdomen, beginning phase three of the operation. The assistant retracts the dorsal mesenteric attachments of the terminal ileum anteriorly, while the surgeon incises the retroperitoneal attachments of the terminal ileum near the cecum, dissecting cephalad and leftward toward the duodenum. The ileocolic and right colic arteries are identified on the dorsal mesentery surface, and windows are created on either side of the vessels. The ileocolic artery is ligated and divided with an endoscopic vascular stapler. The middle colic vessels are identified, cleared, and ligated, allowing division of the transverse mesocolon. The omentum is separated from the proximal transverse colon, and the right colon and hepatic flexure are detached from the retroperitoneum. The colon is moved into the pelvis for extraction through a suprapubic incision. The ileum is divided, and a circular stapler anvil is secured with a purse string suture. The ileum is returned to the abdomen, and the ileo-rectal anastomosis is formed using a double stapling technique. The anastomosis is checked for leaks by air insufflation of the rectum.
